skill.name: vercel-react-best-practices
skill.description: Performance-focused React and Next.js guidance for agents that write, review, or refactor application code. It covers data-fetching waterfalls, bundle size, server rendering, client fetching, rerenders, rendering cost, and JavaScript hot paths.
skill.summary: Apply Vercel-style React and Next.js performance rules during coding, reviews, and refactors.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many detections, but most are false positives caused by markdown code fences, inline code, reference links, and instructional React examples. I found no prompt injection or malicious skill execution path. Publication should wait for content changes because one example teaches logging a session cookie and another promotes an inline script pattern that needs stronger security caveats.

high_findings.title: Sensitive Session Cookie Logging in Recommended Example
high_findings.description: The server-after example reads a session-id cookie and passes it to logUserAction. This can teach agents to write session identifiers into logs, which can expose credentials through observability systems or log exports.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly reads a session-id cookie and logs it. It is documentation rather than executable skill code, so the confidence is high for unsafe guidance but not for malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Inline Script Guidance Uses React Dangerous HTML Sink
medium_findings.description: The hydration example recommends dangerouslySetInnerHTML with a synchronous script that reads localStorage. The shown value is not sent over the network, but this pattern can weaken CSP and become an XSS sink if adapted with untrusted data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous sink and browser storage read are explicit in the example. The example is narrow and does not interpolate remote input, so the risk depends on how agents reuse it.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Most external command detections are markdown code fences, inline identifiers, or local development commands such as pnpm build. They are documentation content and are not executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are markdown examples, headings, or inline code. I found no evidence that the skill invokes shell commands during use.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Dynamic Imports Are Performance Examples
low_findings.description: The dynamic import detections are examples showing lazy loading for React and Next.js components. They use fixed module paths and do not load attacker-controlled code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All reviewed import expressions appear inside fenced instructional examples. The paths are hardcoded package or local module examples, not user-controlled expressions.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: Network URL detections point to React, Next.js, SWR, Vercel, and GitHub reference links. They are not outbound requests or exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations contain static reference URLs and attribution links. I found no evidence that the skill performs network requests itself.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Crypto and Key Detections Are Keyword Collisions
low_findings.description: Weak cryptography and certificate/key findings are caused by terms such as md, impact, key, or markdown filenames in documentation. No cryptographic operation or certificate material was found in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are prose or ordinary TypeScript examples, not crypto code or secret files. The scanner appears to match short tokens and variable names without semantic context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive Session Cookie Logging in Recommended Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The server-after example reads a session-id cookie and passes it to logUserAction. This can teach agents to write session identifiers into logs, which can expose credentials through observability systems or log exports.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly reads a session-id cookie and logs it. It is documentation rather than executable skill code, so the confidence is high for unsafe guidance but not for malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Inline Script Guidance Uses React Dangerous HTML Sink
dangerous_patterns.description: The hydration example recommends dangerouslySetInnerHTML with a synchronous script that reads localStorage. The shown value is not sent over the network, but this pattern can weaken CSP and become an XSS sink if adapted with untrusted data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous sink and browser storage read are explicit in the example. The example is narrow and does not interpolate remote input, so the risk depends on how agents reuse it.

content.value_statement: React and Next.js apps often lose speed through waterfalls, oversized bundles, and avoidable rerenders. This skill gives agents concrete rules for performance-focused code reviews and refactors.
actual_capabilities: Identifies sequential awaits and recommends parallel data-fetching patterns.
Guides bundle reduction with direct imports, dynamic imports, and delayed third-party loading.
Explains server-side React patterns such as Suspense, React.cache, and non-blocking after hooks.
Reviews client data-fetching patterns for SWR deduplication and shared event subscriptions.
Suggests rerender and rendering optimizations for state dependencies, memoization, lists, and SVGs.
Provides rule-specific examples in individual markdown files and a compiled AGENTS.md guide.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not run automated benchmarks or profiling tools.
Some examples need security review before copying into production applications.
Recommendations may need adjustment for projects using older React or Next.js versions.
It does not replace application-specific architecture review or accessibility testing.

use_cases.title: Review Pull Requests for Performance
use_cases.description: Find waterfalls, heavy imports, repeated storage reads, and avoidable rerenders before code is merged.

use_cases.title: Refactor Slow Next.js Pages
use_cases.description: Apply server component composition, Suspense boundaries, and parallel fetching to improve response and paint times.

use_cases.title: Teach Agent Coding Standards
use_cases.description: Give Claude, Codex, or Claude Code repeatable rules for generating performant React patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Performance Review
prompt_templates.scenario: A beginner wants a focused review of one component or page.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this React or Next.js file using the Vercel React best practices. List the highest-impact performance issues first.

prompt_templates.title: Fix Data Fetching Waterfalls
prompt_templates.scenario: A developer suspects slow server rendering or API route latency.
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect this Next.js route or server component for async waterfalls. Refactor independent work to run in parallel where appropriate.

prompt_templates.title: Reduce Client Bundle Size
prompt_templates.scenario: A team wants to reduce initial JavaScript and improve interaction time.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these imports and client components for bundle size problems. Recommend direct imports, dynamic imports, and deferred libraries.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Full-Stack Optimization Pass
prompt_templates.scenario: An experienced engineer wants broad performance guidance across a feature.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this React and Next.js feature for server, client, rendering, and JavaScript hot-path performance. Include risk, tradeoffs, and test suggestions.

output_examples.input: A Next.js page awaits user data before rendering the layout and sidebar.
output: Move independent layout and sidebar work into separate server components.
Start independent requests before awaiting dependent data.
Use a Suspense boundary only around content that can stream later.

output_examples.input: A component imports many icons and a heavy editor in the first render path.
output: Replace broad library imports with direct icon imports or Next.js optimized package imports.
Load the editor dynamically when the user opens the editing workflow.
Consider preloading on hover or focus when the editor is likely to be used.

output_examples.input: A client component rerenders on every window width change and reads storage repeatedly.
output: Subscribe to a derived media query boolean instead of raw width.
Cache synchronous storage reads when values are reused.
Narrow effect dependencies to primitive values that actually change behavior.

best_practices: Start with critical waterfall and bundle-size rules before applying smaller JavaScript optimizations.
Verify examples against your project version, security policy, and user experience requirements.
Use profiling, bundle analysis, and focused tests to confirm that a refactor improved real behavior.
anti_patterns: Do not apply every micro-optimization before measuring the highest-impact bottlenecks.
Do not copy inline script or cookie logging examples without a security review.
Do not use Suspense boundaries where the loading state would cause unacceptable layout shift.

faq.question: Does this skill modify code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance for an agent that may then edit code when the user asks.

faq.question: Is it only for Next.js projects?
faq.answer: No. Many React rendering, state, and JavaScript performance rules apply outside Next.js.

faq.question: Does it require Vercel hosting?
faq.answer: No. The guidance references Vercel practices, but most rules apply to any React or Next.js deployment.

faq.question: Can it replace performance profiling?
faq.answer: No. It helps find common issues, but benchmarks and profiling should confirm important changes.

faq.question: Are all examples safe to copy?
faq.answer: No. Examples involving cookies, browser storage, or inline scripts need project-specific security review.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
